title: MCP server process has no Django dead-DB-connection hygiene; ORM on asyncio.to_thread pool threads in refresh/view-schema builds also unguarded
claim: The 2026-06-09 22h-outage mechanism (long-lived Django process reuses a dead connection forever after an RDS maintenance window) was fixed only for procrastinate tasks via the TEMPORARY decorator. The MCP server is an identical long-lived Django-ORM process (django.setup, per-tool-call ORM atouch/ThreadJob writes) with zero close_old_connections, no CONN_MAX_AGE/CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS, so after the next RDS restart every ORM-touching MCP tool call fails until process restart — the agent loses query/list_tables/run_materialization wholesale. The decorator's cleanup also only reaches the async-ORM sensitive thread; build_view_schema (heavy sync ORM) runs via asyncio.to_thread at two sites and refresh_tenant_schema runs the entire ORM-writing run_pipeline via to_thread, all on default-executor pool threads with no guard — a pool thread holding a connection that died poisons view-schema rebuilds (surfacing as 'system-side fix required') and refreshes, and the FAILED-persist save() on the same dead connection also raises, leaving the row stuck PROVISIONING.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain and reproduced every link.

1) THE FIX IS PROCRASTINATE-ONLY. config/procrastinate.py:17-77 — the only dead-connection hygiene in the repo. `_acleanup_before = sync_to_async(close_old_connections, thread_sensitive=True)` (line 31) runs on asgiref's thread-sensitive executor — the same thread async ORM routes to. The docstring itself scopes it: "the worker is a long-lived process". A repo-wide grep for `close_old_connections|CONN_MAX_AGE|CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS` returns hits ONLY in config/procrastinate.py and apps/workspaces/tasks.py:483 — nowhere else. config/settings/base.py:120-122 defines DATABASES with no CONN_MAX_AGE/CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS; I grepped all settings modules — none set them.

2) MCP IS AN UNGUARDED LONG-LIVED DJANGO-ORM PROCESS. mcp_server/__main__.py:13 and mcp_server/server.py:894 call `django.setup()`; server.py:914 `mcp.run(transport=...)` is a persistent FastMCP loop, started as a standalone process (docker-compose.yml:25 `python -m mcp_server --transport streamable-http`; Procfile.dev:2). It does per-tool-call ORM: `ts.atouch()` / `vs.atouch()` (mcp_server/context.py:66,125) on EVERY query/list_tables/get_metadata/describe_table call via load_tenant_context/load_workspace_context; `atouch` issues an ORM UPDATE (models.py:53-56 `await self.asave(update_fields=["last_accessed_at"])`). run_materialization does `ThreadJob.objects.acreate` (server.py:623); cancel_materialization does `run.asave` (server.py:482). I grepped mcp_server/ for request_started/request_finished/close_old_connections/lifespan/on_startup — ZERO. FastMCP's streamable-http transport is plain Starlette/ASGI; it does NOT run Django's request signals (those only fire via Django's own handler, which MCP doesn't use). So after an RDS restart the long-lived MCP connection is reused-closed-forever and every ORM-touching tool call fails until process restart — exactly the June-2026 mechanism, just in a process the decorator never reaches.

3) to_thread POOL THREADS ARE UNGUARDED EXCEPT ONE SITE. tasks.py:478-483 documents that asyncio.to_thread executor threads have their own thread-local Django connection "that the task decorator's close_old_connections ... cannot reach", and adds `close_old_connections()` at line 483 — but ONLY inside `_run_pipeline_with_progress` (used by materialize_workspace at tasks.py:277). The other to_thread sites have NO such guard: refresh_tenant_schema calls `run_pipeline` DIRECTLY via to_thread (tasks.py:173, NOT _run_pipeline_with_progress), and run_pipeline does heavy ORM writes (mcp_server/services/materializer.py: MaterializationRun.objects.create:186, .update:240/393/419/435/471/617, run.save:370, tenant_schema.save:486, TenantMetadata.objects.update_or_create:539). build_view_schema runs via to_thread at tasks.py:324 (materialize_workspace) and tasks.py:571 (rebuild_workspace_view_schema) with no guard; its ORM portion (schema_manager.py:265 TenantSchema.objects.filter, :280 get_or_create, :287/430/438 vs.save, :306 Tenant.objects.get) uses the Django default connection on the pool thread. The FAILED-persist `vs.save(update_fields=["state","last_error"])` at schema_manager.py:430 runs on that same possibly-dead connection, so it raises too — leaving the WorkspaceViewSchema stuck in PROVISIONING (state set at :286/287 before the try), exactly as the finding claims.

4) REACHABILITY — all paths live today: MCP tools (query/list_tables/get_metadata/run_materialization etc.) are @mcp.tool() decorated and are the agent's data-access surface. refresh_tenant_schema is deferred from a live DRF endpoint (apps/workspaces/api/views.py:365, role+membership gated). rebuild_workspace_view_schema is deferred on tenant add/remove (workspace_service.py:28,71) and sibling rebuild (tasks.py:456). build_view_schema also runs inline in materialize_workspace, which is dispatched by the run_materialization MCP tool.
votes.notes: CONFIRMED. Only nuance: build_view_schema's DDL uses a FRESH psycopg connection per call (schema_manager.py:43 `get_managed_db_connection` -> psycopg.connect), so the DDL itself is NOT at risk from the dead-connection-reuse bug — but build_view_schema's interleaved Django *ORM* calls (and the FAILED-persist vs.save at :430) ARE on the reused default connection, which is the exact path the finding names for the stuck-PROVISIONING outcome, so the conclusion stands. Severity/status LATENT is correct: this is dormant until the next RDS maintenance/idle-timeout event, at which point it reproduces the 22h-class outage in the MCP process (agent loses query/list_tables/run_materialization wholesale) and in the unguarded refresh/rebuild task paths. The one-line "two sites" / "entire run_pipeline via to_thread" descriptions are both accurate.

title: Checkpointer pool singleton: unsynchronized init race; force_new closes the shared pool under concurrent streams; no borrow-time health check
claim: apps/chat/checkpointer.py builds a module-global AsyncConnectionPool + AsyncPostgresSaver with no lock: two concurrent cold-start requests interleave (the second closes the first's half-open pool, failing its setup); chat_view retries ANY build_agent_graph exception with ensure_checkpointer(force_new=True), which closes the pool that all other in-flight chat streams in that uvicorn worker are using for checkpoint writes — one request's transient error degrades every concurrent conversation; the pool is created without check= so dead pooled connections are handed out after a DB blip. AsyncPostgresSaver.setup() also runs at first use in every process — LangGraph checkpoint DDL applied at runtime outside Django migration discipline. With 4 workers x max_size 20, checkpointing alone can hold up to 80 platform-DB connections beside ORM and the worker, against db.t4g.micro's ~110 cap.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced every link from the live route to the consequence.

(1) Module-global singleton, no synchronization. apps/chat/checkpointer.py:14-15 declares `_checkpointer = None` / `_pool = None` at module scope; line 19 `global _checkpointer, _pool`. Searched the whole chat app: `grep -rn "Lock|Semaphore" apps/chat/` returns nothing, and checkpointer.py imports no `asyncio`. No `check=` kwarg on the pool. Confirmed.

(2) Cold-start init race. ensure_checkpointer guard is `if _checkpointer is not None and not force_new: return` (line 20). On cold start `_checkpointer is None`, so two concurrent requests both fall through. The body has multiple awaits (`await _pool.open(... timeout=10)` line 38, `await _checkpointer.setup()` line 41) that yield the event loop. Since `_pool`/`_checkpointer` are shared globals, an interleaved second request hits `if _pool is not None: await _pool.close()` (lines 26-27) and closes the first request's pool object before the first's `setup()` (line 41) runs against it. Confirmed mechanism.

(3) force_new closes the pool other in-flight streams use. chat_view wraps the first `ensure_checkpointer()` + `build_agent_graph()` in a bare `except Exception:` (views.py:174) and retries with `ensure_checkpointer(force_new=True)` (views.py:178). With force_new, line 27 `await _pool.close()` closes the SHARED module-global pool. Other concurrent requests already built their agent (apps/agents/graph/base.py:657 `graph.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)`) capturing the OLD AsyncPostgresSaver instance, which references the OLD pool object — the exact object force_new just closed. Those agents write checkpoints via that pool throughout streaming (apps/chat/stream.py:79-249 `agent.astream_events`). So one request's transient build error closes the pool under every concurrent conversation in that worker. Confirmed.

(4) Reachability is live. config/urls.py:98 `path("api/chat/", chat_view, ...)` and apps/chat/urls.py:28 both route to chat_view. Production runs it under uvicorn `--workers 4` (config/deploy.yml:17). thread_views.py:97 also calls `ensure_checkpointer()`. Confirmed reachable today.

(5) Runtime DDL outside Django migrations. Inspected the installed library: `AsyncPostgresSaver.setup()` docstring says "creates the necessary tables ... and runs database migrations ... MUST be called ... the first time" and the body executes `self.MIGRATIONS[...]` DDL via a cursor. checkpointer.py:41 calls it at first use in every process. Confirmed.

(6) Connection budget. checkpointer.py:31 `max_size=20`; deploy.yml:17 `--workers 4` => up to 80 persistent pool connections. infra/scout-stack.yml:16 DBInstanceClass default `db.t4g.micro`. Django uses default CONN_MAX_AGE=0 (verified: no CONN_MAX_AGE in any config/settings module, and env.db produces no CONN_MAX_AGE key), so ORM connections are per-request/short-lived and separate from the persistent 80-connection pool budget — and the worker (config/deploy-worker.yml:16 `manage.py procrastinate worker`) is an additional process consuming connections. Budget framing accurate.
votes.notes: All six sub-claims reproduced independently from source; cited file:line references are all accurate. Status LATENT is correct: the catastrophic force_new path only triggers AFTER one request's first ensure_checkpointer()+build_agent_graph() raises (a transient connection/build error), so it does not fire on the warm happy path — but when it does fire under concurrency it degrades every in-flight stream, which is precisely what LATENT (armed, fires under a specific trigger) captures. Impact correctness is right (checkpoint writes fail / conversation persistence broken for concurrent users); there is also a real cost-perf dimension (up to 80 persistent connections vs db.t4g.micro's ~110-112 cap, beside ORM + worker), but correctness is the more severe and primary axis, so I would not downgrade. Minor nuance, not a refutation: the bare `except Exception:` at views.py:174 means force_new is triggered by ANY build failure (MCP/graph/auth errors too), not only connection staleness — which actually widens the blast radius beyond what a strict reading of the comment ('Connection may have gone stale') implies, strengthening rather than weakening the finding. No defensive code (lock, check=, per-stream pool ownership, or scoped retry) exists to mitigate any of this.

title: Single worker process at concurrency 1: all background work serialized platform-wide; worker deploy on every merge kills in-flight jobs
claim: deploy-worker.yml runs bare 'procrastinate worker' with no --concurrency (default 1) and no queue, so all materializations, teardown/rebuild tasks, thread resumes, and both periodic janitors serialize through one slot — a long materialization blocks the TTL janitor and ThreadJob janitor entirely and delays every other workspace's work by its full duration (and inflates the created_at-anchored staleness that feeds the false-fail-resume race). tasks.py:368-370 relies on one-task-at-a-time for a timing argument, so the concurrency=1 assumption is load-bearing and unpinned. Every push to main redeploys the worker via Kamal with no stop_wait_time and procrastinate's shutdown_graceful_timeout defaults to None, so a running materialization is SIGKILLed after docker's grace — the deploy-triggered re-arm of the zombie-doing incident class.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced every link of the chain against the actual files:

CONCURRENCY=1, ALL QUEUES, ONE SLOT:
- config/deploy-worker.yml:16 — the worker command is exactly `cmd: "python manage.py procrastinate worker"`. No `--concurrency`, no `--queues`, no `-q`. This is the only worker server defined (single host: `SCOUT_EC2_IP`, one process).
- Procrastinate is pinned at 3.8.1 (uv.lock). In `.venv/.../procrastinate/worker.py:28` `WORKER_CONCURRENCY = 1`, and `worker.py:39` `concurrency: int = WORKER_CONCURRENCY`. The CLI (`cli.py:288-291`) declares `--concurrency` with envvar `WORKER_CONCURRENCY` and NO argparse default, so when unset it is omitted from kwargs and the worker falls to its own default of 1. I grepped config/, apps/, .env.example, Procfile* for `WORKER_CONCURRENCY` overrides — none exist.
- The serialization is global, not per-queue: `worker.py:79` and `:595` create a single `self._job_semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(self.concurrency)`. With concurrency=1 that is one global slot across all queues — exactly one task body runs at a time.
- No task sets a queue. `config/procrastinate.py` `task()` wrapper just forwards `**task_kwargs` to `app.task(**task_kwargs)`; I grepped `queue=` across apps/, config/, mcp_server/ and found ZERO occurrences. So materializations, teardowns, view-schema rebuilds, resume tasks, and both janitors all land on the default queue and contend for the same single slot.

BOTH JANITORS ARE PERIODIC TASKS ON THIS WORKER, AND BLOCKED BY IT:
- apps/workspaces/tasks.py:516 `@app.periodic(cron="*/30 * * * *")` → `expire_inactive_schemas` (the TTL janitor; dispatches `teardown_schema`).
- apps/workspaces/tasks.py:819 `@app.periodic(cron="*/15 * * * *")` → `expire_stale_thread_jobs` (the ThreadJob janitor). Its query is `created_at__lt=cutoff` (tasks.py:833) — confirming the finding's "created_at-anchored staleness" detail. Periodic tasks are deferred onto the same default queue, so a long-running materialization holds the only slot and the janitor jobs sit queued (cannot run) until it completes.

MATERIALIZATION RUNS ON THIS WORKER:
- mcp_server/server.py:607 `job = await materialize_workspace.defer_async(...)`, defined at tasks.py:203-204 `@task(pass_context=True) async def materialize_workspace`. So the user-facing materialization path is dispatched to this single-slot worker.

LOAD-BEARING concurrency=1 ASSUMPTION, UNPINNED:
- tasks.py:368-370 (inside `_defer_resume_for_job` docstring): "Hedge with a bounded backoff: total budget ~3.75s, which is acceptable because procrastinate workers handle one task at a time per slot." Confirmed verbatim. The timing argument explicitly depends on concurrency=1, yet concurrency is never pinned anywhere.

DEPLOY-ON-EVERY-MERGE + SIGKILL:
- .github/workflows/deploy.yml:4-5 `on: push: branches: [main]`; step at :79-80 `Deploy Worker: kamal deploy -c config/deploy-worker.yml`. Every merge to main redeploys the worker.
- No `stop_wait_time`, `drain`, or `stop`/`boot` override in deploy-worker.yml (grep returned nothing), so Docker's default ~10s stop grace applies.
- procrastinate default `shutdown_graceful_timeout: float | None = None` (worker.py:43, :81). On SIGTERM the worker calls `self.stop()` (worker.py:602 `signals.on_stop(self.stop)`), then at worker.py:496-499 does `asyncio.wait(self._running_jobs, timeout=self.shutdown_graceful_timeout)` — timeout=None means it waits INDEFINITELY for the in-flight materialization. Because procrastinate won't self-abort within Docker's grace window, Docker sends SIGKILL after ~10s, hard-killing the in-flight materialization. That is precisely the "deploy-triggered re-arm of the zombie-doing incident class" the finding describes (and which the codebase's own MEMORY references as the materialization orphan-jobs / zombie-`doing` incident class).
votes.notes:
All four reviewer lenses' core claims reproduced independently. Severity classification (DEBT / cost-perf) is appropriate: this is a real serialization-and-deploy-fragility weakness rather than a present-tense outage, and it amplifies (does not by itself cause) the known false-fail-resume race via inflated created_at-anchored staleness while the slot is held.

One precision on mechanism, fully consistent with the finding's wording: procrastinate with shutdown_graceful_timeout=None does NOT itself abort the running job on SIGTERM (it would wait forever); the SIGKILL is delivered by Docker/Kamal after the container stop grace (default ~10s, no stop_wait_time override). So the kill is real and deploy-triggered, but the proximate killer is Docker, with procrastinate's None default being what prevents an orderly in-window shutdown. The finding states this correctly.

Reachability: live. deploy.yml fires on every push to main; run_materialization is a live MCP tool (mcp_server/server.py:521) that user chat invokes; both janitors are registered @app.periodic and run on this same single-slot worker. Nothing is dead code or behind a flag.

title: No .dockerignore: documented manual-deploy path bakes developer .env/.env.deploy into the production image; base.py loads /app/.env at runtime
claim: Dockerfile does COPY . . with no .dockerignore, and DEPLOYMENT.md documents local 'kamal setup/deploy' as a supported path — a dev checkout's .env (real API keys, Fernet key), .env.deploy (RDS endpoint/ARNs), .git, node_modules etc. are copied into ECR image layers. base.py:24-26 reads /app/.env in every container; read_env doesn't overwrite Kamal-injected vars, but any var the Kamal env blocks omit (SOCIALACCOUNT_ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS, DEFAULT_LLM_MODEL, CONNECT_API_URL, OCS_URL, AGENT_RESUME_*) is silently sourced from whoever's laptop built the image. CI builds are clean.

votes.evidence:
CONFIRMED components (re-traced independently):
1. No .dockerignore anywhere: `ls /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/.dockerignore` and `frontend/.dockerignore` both return "No such file or directory".
2. Dockerfile:29 is `COPY . .` (verified by reading /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/Dockerfile).
3. config/settings/base.py:24-26 reads a baked .env at runtime: `env_file = BASE_DIR / ".env"` / `if env_file.exists(): env.read_env(str(env_file))`. django-environ read_env defaults to overwrite=False, so Kamal-injected vars win — matching the finding's mechanism description.
4. config/deploy.yml env blocks (clear: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE, DJANGO_ALLOWED_HOSTS, CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS, MCP_SERVER_URL, etc.; secret: DATABASE_URL, DJANGO_SECRET_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, etc.) indeed omit SOCIALACCOUNT_ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS (base.py:247), DEFAULT_LLM_MODEL (base.py:281), CONNECT_API_URL (base.py:313), OCS_URL (base.py:314), AGENT_RESUME_TIMEOUT_S/RECURSION_LIMIT (base.py:287, 351) — all read via env() with code defaults.
5. DEPLOYMENT.md "Manual Deployment" section (~lines 150-195) documents `gem install kamal`, `kamal setup`, `kamal deploy` from a local machine, and the dev checkout does contain untracked .env, .env.deploy, .env.langfuse (all gitignored per .gitignore "Secrets" block).
6. CI (.github/workflows/deploy.yml) builds via `docker build .` from a fresh actions/checkout — no .env can exist there, so "CI builds are clean" is correct.

REFUTED component (the headline mechanism):
The claim that local `kamal setup`/`kamal deploy` "bakes a dev checkout's .env/.env.deploy into ECR image layers" is wrong for this repo. Kamal 2's default build context is NOT the working directory — per official builder docs (kamal-deploy.org/docs/configuration/builders): "If this is not set, then a local Git clone of the repo is used. This ensures a clean build with no uncommitted changes." None of the four configs (config/deploy.yml:59-60, deploy-worker.yml:55-56, deploy-mcp.yml:41-42, deploy-frontend.yml:26-30) sets `builder.context`, so the clone default applies. .env/.env.deploy/.env.langfuse and frontend/node_modules are gitignored+untracked, hence absent from any git clone and cannot enter the build context. A stale Kamal 1 (which did build from cwd) is not plausible: the configs use Kamal-2-only syntax (`proxy: false` at deploy.yml:18, `builder.arch` rather than `multiarch`), which Kamal 1 rejects, and DEPLOYMENT.md:177 instructs plain `gem install kamal` (2.x since 2024).

What residually remains true: (a) with no .dockerignore, `COPY . .` does bake the `.git` directory (full committed history) plus docs/tests/.kamal into every image in BOTH paths (CI checkout and Kamal clone both contain .git) — image bloat and history exposure inside private ECR, committed content only, no laptop secrets; (b) the .env-baking hazard exists one undocumented step away — anyone running raw `docker build . && docker push` from a dev checkout (the CI workflow's exact command, run locally) would bake .env, and base.py:24-26 would then silently consume it. A .dockerignore is the standard guardrail and is genuinely missing.
votes.notes: Verdict is PARTIAL, not REFUTED, because the missing .dockerignore is a real defect with real (lesser) consequences — .git/history baked into all production images, and zero defense-in-depth if anyone ever mimics CI's `docker build .` locally. But the finding's central exploitation chain is unreachable as stated: the documented manual path (kamal setup/deploy) builds from a clean git clone under Kamal 2, so developer .env/.env.deploy never reach the image, and therefore the "vars silently sourced from whoever's laptop" runtime consequence via base.py never triggers via any documented path today. Downgrade from LATENT (one config-change/mistake away with active runtime sink) to DEBT (missing standard guardrail; recommend adding .dockerignore covering .env*, .git, node_modules, docs, tests). The base.py /app/.env runtime-read behavior is worth removing in production settings regardless, as it converts any future context mistake into silent config injection.

votes.evidence:
CONFIRMED pieces (re-traced independently):
1. No .dockerignore exists anywhere in the repo (`find . -name .dockerignore` → nothing), and /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/Dockerfile:29 is `COPY . .`. The dev checkout root contains untracked `.env` (1508 bytes) and `.env.deploy` (633 bytes); both are gitignored (.gitignore:41-43) and not in `git ls-files`.
2. /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/config/settings/base.py:24-26: `env_file = BASE_DIR / ".env"; if env_file.exists(): env.read_env(str(env_file))` — BASE_DIR resolves to /app in the container, so any baked .env would be read at runtime. django-environ's signature (site-packages/environ/environ.py:913) is `read_env(cls, env_file=None, overwrite=False, ...)`, so Kamal-injected vars win but omitted vars fall through to a baked file.
3. The five named vars are genuinely absent from every Kamal env block: `grep SOCIALACCOUNT_ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS|DEFAULT_LLM_MODEL|CONNECT_API_URL|OCS_URL|AGENT_RESUME config/deploy*.yml .kamal/secrets` → no hits, while base.py reads them with defaults at lines 247, 281, 287, 313-314, 351.
4. CI (.github/workflows/deploy.yml:44-63) builds with raw `docker build` from a fresh `actions/checkout` — no .env present, so CI images carry no secrets. They DO bake the `.git` directory (checkout leaves it; no .dockerignore excludes it) — bloat/repo-history in ECR, not credentials.

REFUTED piece — the headline mechanism:
The documented manual path (DEPLOYMENT.md:149-192, "Manual Deployment": `gem install kamal`, then `kamal setup`/`kamal deploy`) does NOT bake the developer's .env/.env.deploy. `gem install kamal` installs Kamal 2.x (2.11.0 verified locally), and Kamal 2 builds from a scrubbed git clone, not the working directory: kamal-2.11.0/lib/kamal/configuration/builder.rb:138-140 (`git_clone?` true when in a git repo and `builder_config["context"].nil?` — Scout's config/deploy.yml:59-60 builder block sets only `arch: amd64`, no `context`), build_directory = $TMPDIR/kamal-clones/... (builder.rb:142-150), and lib/kamal/commands/builder/clone.rb `clone_reset_steps` runs `git reset --hard <revision>` + `git clean -fdx`. Untracked/gitignored files (.env, .env.deploy, .venv, node_modules) therefore never enter the build context. Only an undocumented raw `docker build && docker push` from a dev checkout would bake secrets.
votes.notes: The missing .dockerignore is a real hardening gap worth fixing: (a) every CI image bakes `.git` (history bloat in ECR layers); (b) the base.py /app/.env runtime-read plus overwrite=False fall-through is a live booby trap — if anyone ever builds with a raw `docker build` from a dev checkout and pushes (debugging, a future workflow change, or downgrading to Kamal 1.x which built from cwd), secrets and silently-sourced config (the five unmanaged vars) would ship exactly as the finding describes. But today, both wired paths are clean: CI checkouts have no .env, and the documented manual Kamal 2.x path builds from a `git clean -fdx` clone. So the finding's "documented manual-deploy path bakes developer .env into the production image" claim is wrong about current behavior — status is DEBT (defense-in-depth gap, no reachable leak path under documented workflows), not LATENT-on-the-documented-path. Fix is cheap: add .dockerignore (.env*, .git, node_modules, .venv, tests, docs) and consider dropping the runtime .env read in production settings.

title: Deploys not gated on tests; migration ordering by convention; labs migrations opt-in default-off; version-skew windows
claim: ci.yml runs only on pull_request; deploy.yml triggers on push to main with no CI dependency, so direct pushes/admin-merges/jointly-broken PR pairs deploy untested, and CI lacks makemigrations --check. Migrations run at API-container boot, but deploy order is MCP -> API -> Worker, so new MCP code runs against the old schema until the API boots and the old worker runs against the new schema until step 3, with no documented rollback for a mid-pipeline failure; DEPLOYMENT.md claims a pre-deploy migration hook but .kamal/hooks/pre-deploy is 'exit 0'. deploy-labs.yml's run_migrations input defaults to false, so deploying a backend image with model changes while unchecked ships code against an unmigrated DB with nothing detecting it.

votes.evidence:
I re-read all six cited files end-to-end and independently reproduced every sub-claim.

1) CI runs only on PRs, deploy on push to main, no gate:
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml:3-6` — `on: pull_request: branches:[main]` only. Jobs are lint-python (ruff), lint-frontend (bun lint), test (pytest). No push trigger.
- `.github/workflows/deploy.yml:3-6` — `on: push: branches:[main]` + `workflow_dispatch`. I grepped deploy.yml for `workflow_run`/`needs:`/`if: success` — zero matches; no inline test/lint steps. The two workflows are fully independent, so direct pushes/admin-merges/jointly-broken-PR-pairs deploy without CI ever running or passing. (Only possible mitigation, required-status-check branch protection, is GitHub repo config, not auditable from the repo.)

2) CI lacks makemigrations --check: grep for `makemigrations` across `.github/` and `pyproject.toml/setup.cfg/pytest.ini/tox.ini/conftest.py` returned nothing.

3) Migration ordering / version-skew:
- `docker-entrypoint.sh:7-9` runs `python manage.py migrate --no-input` ONLY when `$1 == "uvicorn"`.
- Kamal CMDs confirm only the API is uvicorn: `config/deploy.yml:17` cmd=`uvicorn ...`; `config/deploy-mcp.yml:17` cmd=`python -m mcp_server ...`; `config/deploy-worker.yml:16` cmd=`python manage.py procrastinate worker`. So MCP and worker boots do NOT migrate.
- `deploy.yml:73-83` deploy order is MCP(73-74) -> API(76-77) -> Worker(79-80) -> Frontend(82-83). Thus new MCP code serves against the old schema until the API migrates, and the old worker runs against the new schema until step 3. Windows are real (and short). Nothing enforces expand/contract; a mid-pipeline failure (e.g. API step fails after MCP succeeded) leaves mixed versions with no documented rollback — I found no rollback step anywhere in deploy.yml.

4) DEPLOYMENT.md vs pre-deploy hook:
- `DEPLOYMENT.md:35` states step 4 = "Runs migrations in a pre-deploy hook (API service only)".
- `.kamal/hooks/pre-deploy:8` is literally `exit 0`, with a comment (lines 4-6) explicitly saying migrations are handled in docker-entrypoint.sh, NOT the hook, because the hook runs before Kamal syncs the env file. So the DEPLOYMENT.md statement is factually wrong about the mechanism (migrations still happen, just at container boot, not in the hook).

5) Labs migrations opt-in default-off:
- `.github/workflows/deploy-labs.yml:15-19` — `run_migrations` boolean input, `default: false`. The migrate step (`:227-228`) is gated `if: inputs.run_migrations == true` and runs an ECS `run-task` with command override `python manage.py migrate --noinput` (`:240`, container name `api`). With the box unchecked, a backend image with model changes ships against an unmigrated DB; the auto-detect logic (`:85-128`) only guarantees a fresh image, nothing detects pending migrations — failure occurs at runtime, not boot.

Reachability: all confirmed live. `deploy.yml` is actively maintained (recent commits: Sentry source maps, concurrency group, env-var wiring) and is the real production path (all referenced `config/deploy*.yml` exist). `docker-entrypoint.sh` is the Dockerfile ENTRYPOINT (`Dockerfile:41`) used by both prod and labs (labs reuses the same `Dockerfile` per deploy-labs.yml build-backend). `ci.yml` runs on every PR. `deploy-labs.yml` is workflow_dispatch, recently touched (`14ff322 ci(labs): deploy scout worker...`).
votes.notes:
CONFIRMED as a bundle; status DEBT and impact correctness are appropriate. Two precision notes that do not change the verdict:

(a) The labs sub-claim's runtime-failure outcome depends on whether the labs web ECS task runs the default Docker CMD (`uvicorn`, which would trigger entrypoint migrations on boot and partially backstop the unchecked toggle) or overrides the command. The labs ECS task definitions live OUTSIDE this repo (`labs-jj-cluster`); I could not repo-confirm. The existence of a dedicated `run_migrations` toggle + a separate ECS migrate task with an explicit command override strongly implies the labs web service is NOT the migrator there, which makes the "unmigrated DB at runtime" mechanism plausible — but it is inference, not repo-proven. The source review (lens-ops-config.md:87) itself flags this as 'unauditable from here', so the finding is honest about the limit.

(b) The DEPLOYMENT.md defect is a documentation inaccuracy (wrong location named for migrations), NOT a functional gap — migrations DO run, via the entrypoint, on the production API container. So the 'pre-deploy migration hook' claim being false is real but its consequence is operator confusion / missing rollback guidance, not missing migrations in prod.

(c) Minor unrelated doc drift spotted while tracing: docker-entrypoint.sh:3 comment and DEPLOYMENT.md:23 both say 'Celery worker', but the worker CMD is `procrastinate worker`. Not part of this finding; noted for completeness.

The replication=2 and the two listed reviewers (lens-ops-config, lens-test-architecture) are consistent with the merged source report content I located at docs/arch-review/2026-06-12/reports/lens-ops-config.md:81,87,165.

title: DEPLOY_ENVIRONMENT heuristic mislabels connectlabs as 'development' for Sentry/Task Badger; three hand-maintained env blocks drift (MCP lacks Task Badger/Langfuse keys)
claim: base.py sets DEPLOY_ENVIRONMENT='production' only when the settings module ends with '.production', but connectlabs.py inherits production posture with a module name ending '.connectlabs', so Sentry/Task Badger default to environment='development' for labs unless ECS task defs override (unverifiable). The three backend Kamal env blocks (api/worker/mcp) each hand-list vars and drift: deploy-mcp.yml omits TASKBADGER_API_KEY and Langfuse keys, yet mcp_server defers materialize_workspace — so agent-initiated materializations (the primary path) are invisible to Task Badger while API/worker-deferred tasks are tracked. No shared env manifest; each gap is discovered by outage. Server entrypoints (asgi/wsgi/manage) also setdefault development settings (DEBUG=True), so a prod container losing the settings env var silently boots permissive instead of crashing (only the MCP entrypoint fails fast).

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced all four sub-claims by reading the files and running the taskbadger integration.

1. DEPLOY_ENVIRONMENT heuristic (base.py:41-45): DEPLOY_ENVIRONMENT = "production" if os.environ.get("DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE","").endswith(".production") else "development". connectlabs deploys with DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=config.settings.connectlabs (module ends in ".connectlabs", not ".production") → defaults to "development". Confirmed neither production.py nor connectlabs.py override DEPLOY_ENVIRONMENT (grep returned none). SENTRY_ENVIRONMENT (base.py:299) and TASKBADGER_ENVIRONMENT (base.py:307) default to DEPLOY_ENVIRONMENT. grep for connectlabs across *.yml/*.tf/*.json/*.sh found ZERO in-repo deploy manifests, so any SENTRY_ENVIRONMENT override for labs lives in out-of-repo infra — correctly hedged as "unverifiable."

2. Task Badger MCP blind spot (the load-bearing claim) — CONFIRMED via execution. Read .venv/.../taskbadger/procrastinate.py: _wrap_defer (lines 139-160) wraps defer_async so _maybe_create_pending→_create_pending_task→create_task_safe runs AT DEFER TIME in the deferring process; _create_pending_task returns None when not Badger.is_configured() (line 171). The worker-side wrapped() (lines 69-72/88-91) only updates status when TB_TASK_ID_KWARG is present — if the deferrer created no PENDING task, no id is injected and the job runs entirely untracked. I ran a Django shell: materialize_workspace is registered in app.tasks (True), ProcrastinateSystemIntegration instrumentation changes its defer_async (True), _taskbadger_instrumented=True, and system.track_task("materialize_workspace")=True. So it WOULD be tracked if the deferring container had the key. mcp_server/server.py:520 run_materialization is an @mcp.tool() (the agent-facing primary path per its docstring) that defers via materialize_workspace.defer_async (line 607); deploy-mcp.yml secrets list (lines 31-36) has NO TASKBADGER_API_KEY → Badger unconfigured on MCP → agent-initiated materializations are invisible to Task Badger. The only other deferrer is apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:185 (materialization_retry_view, explicitly "without going through the agent"/"retry") running in the API container, whose deploy.yml DOES carry TASKBADGER_API_KEY (line 54). The asymmetry described is exact: primary/agent path untracked, retry path tracked.

3. Three drifting env blocks: deploy.yml (api, has TASKBADGER_API_KEY + Langfuse keys), deploy-worker.yml (has TASKBADGER_API_KEY + Langfuse), deploy-mcp.yml (omits both — grep confirmed neither TASKBADGER nor LANGFUSE present). Each hand-lists vars; no shared manifest. MCP doesn't itself call Langfuse (no references under mcp_server/), so the Langfuse omission is only a symptom of drift, not a functional bug — finding frames it that way correctly.

4. Permissive boot: manage.py:10, config/asgi.py:14, config/wsgi.py:14 all os.environ.setdefault("DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE","config.settings.development") (DEBUG defaults True at base.py:34) → a prod container losing the env var boots permissive silently. Only mcp_server/__main__.py:5-9 raises RuntimeError (fails fast). All confirmed.
votes.notes: Status LATENT and impact velocity are appropriate: materialization still runs correctly; what's lost is observability (Task Badger visibility for the primary agent path, plus the connectlabs Sentry/TaskBadger environment label). No data-loss or correctness consequence. Two minor scoping caveats that do not change the verdict: (a) the connectlabs Sentry mislabel is genuinely unverifiable from this repo since no connectlabs deploy manifest exists in-tree — the finding already says so; (b) the Langfuse-key omission in deploy-mcp.yml has no functional effect because the MCP container never invokes Langfuse — it's evidence of env-block drift, not a runtime gap, and the finding presents it as such. Task Badger also needs org/project slugs (taskbadger.init raised MissingConfiguration when I passed only a token), but that is orthogonal — the integration's defer/run wrapping is gated solely on Badger.is_configured(), which is driven by whether init succeeded; deploy-mcp.yml provides no TB config at all, so the blind spot holds regardless.

title: Share tokens and OAuth codes transit uvicorn/nginx access logs into CloudWatch
claim: Public share endpoints carry the bearer capability in the URL path (/api/chat/threads/shared/<token>/, /api/recipes/runs/shared/<token>/) and OAuth callbacks carry ?code=. Uvicorn's access log is on by default (deploy.yml doesn't disable it; Django LOGGING doesn't govern uvicorn loggers) and frontend nginx logs request lines; both ship to CloudWatch with 30-day retention, so anyone with CloudWatch read access can harvest live share tokens. Not verified against actual prod log output.

votes.evidence: (1) Bearer capability in path, no auth: config/urls.py:101-110 routes "api/recipes/runs/shared/<str:share_token>/" and "api/chat/threads/shared/<str:share_token>/" under "# Public share links (no auth required)"; apps/chat/thread_views.py:227-239 (public_thread_view) does a pure token lookup with zero authentication; tokens are long-lived secrets.token_urlsafe(32) persisted until unshared (apps/chat/models.py:44-47, apps/recipes/models.py:117-120). (2) Uvicorn access log on: config/deploy.yml:17 cmd is "uvicorn config.asgi:application --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --workers 4 --timeout-keep-alive 120 --lifespan off" — no --no-access-log; Django LOGGING (config/settings/production.py:37-73) has "disable_existing_loggers": False and never names uvicorn loggers, so uvicorn.access keeps its default INFO handler logging the full request line (path + query). Grep across config/, Dockerfile, scripts/ found no access-log disabling. (3) Nginx logs same lines: Dockerfile.frontend builds on nginx:alpine (access log → stdout, on by default); frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf has no access_log off and proxies /api/ (line 57) and /accounts/ (line 98). (4) CloudWatch with 30-day retention: config/deploy.yml:62-68 awslogs → /scout/api; config/deploy-frontend.yml:50-56 awslogs → /scout/frontend; infra/scout-stack.yml ApiLogGroup and FrontendLogGroup both RetentionInDays: 30 (lines ~365-387). (5) OAuth ?code=: config/urls.py:83 includes allauth.urls at /accounts/; allauth authorization-code callbacks carry ?code=&state= in the query string captured by both nginx and uvicorn request lines. (6) Reachability: live today — frontend/src/pages/PublicThreadPage.tsx and PublicRecipeRunPage.tsx consume these endpoints; share tokens are generated via the is_shared/is_public model invariants.
votes.notes: Full chain independently reproduced at code/config level; only the actual prod log output was not observed (which the finding itself already discloses). One severity nuance: the OAuth ?code= component is substantially lower value than share tokens — codes are single-use, short-lived, consumed by the legitimate exchange, require the client secret to redeem, and allauth binds state to the session. The materially harvestable credentials are the long-lived share tokens, which remain valid until the owner unshares. Exploitation requires CloudWatch read access (insider/lateral risk), consistent with LATENT. Fix surface: add --no-access-log (or a uvicorn log-config filtering shared/ paths) in config/deploy.yml:17, and access_log off / token-redacting log_format in frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf; alternatively move share tokens out of the URL path.

votes.evidence: Routes: config/urls.py:101-110 registers "api/recipes/runs/shared/<str:share_token>/" and "api/chat/threads/shared/<str:share_token>/" under the comment "# Public share links (no auth required)". Reachability: frontend actively fetches them — frontend/src/pages/PublicThreadPage.tsx:202 `fetch(`${BASE_PATH}/api/chat/threads/shared/${token}/`)` and PublicRecipeRunPage.tsx:63; share creation is wired at apps/chat/urls.py:23 ("<uuid:thread_id>/share/"). OAuth: config/urls.py:83 `path("accounts/", include("allauth.urls"))`; nginx.prod-kamal.conf:98-104 proxies /accounts/, so callback ?code= transits both tiers. Uvicorn: config/deploy.yml:17 `cmd: "uvicorn config.asgi:application --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --workers 4 --timeout-keep-alive 120 --lifespan off"` — no --no-access-log (uvicorn default: access log ON, request line includes path+query). config/settings/production.py:39 `"disable_existing_loggers": False` with no uvicorn.* logger entries, so Django's dictConfig does not silence uvicorn.access. Repo-wide grep for access_log/no-access across config/deploy*.yml, frontend/nginx*.conf, Dockerfile* returns zero hits — nothing disables access logging anywhere. nginx: Dockerfile.frontend:37 `FROM nginx:alpine` (stock image enables http-level access_log to stdout); site config frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf never sets access_log off. CloudWatch: config/deploy.yml:62-68 awslogs driver → group /scout/api; config/deploy-frontend.yml:51-55 → /scout/frontend; infra/scout-stack.yml:365-369 ApiLogGroup RetentionInDays: 30 and :383-387 FrontendLogGroup RetentionInDays: 30. Full chain reproduced: share token in path → logged by nginx and uvicorn request lines → shipped to CloudWatch → readable for 30 days by anyone with logs:GetLogEvents on those groups.
votes.notes: Finding is accurate and slightly understated: there is a third public token-in-path endpoint it missed — /api/recipes/shared/<token>/ (recipe templates), fetched at frontend/src/pages/PublicRecipePage.tsx:55. Caveats: (1) the two steps that rely on upstream defaults (uvicorn access-log-on, nginx:alpine stock access_log→stdout) are well-documented defaults but not provable from repo code alone; the finding itself flags "not verified against actual prod log output" and I likewise could not inspect live CloudWatch. (2) OAuth ?code= exposure is lower value than share tokens (codes are short-lived/single-use and require client secret to redeem); share tokens are durable bearer capabilities, so they are the real payload. (3) Exploitation requires CloudWatch read access (insider/compromised-IAM threat model), consistent with LATENT rather than BROKEN-NOW. IAM policy at infra/scout-stack.yml:183-186 grants the EC2 role access to all four log groups, so any principal with that role or broader logs read can harvest tokens.

title: No detection layer: zero CloudWatch alarms, static health check, no worker heartbeat; process-death journeys emit no operator signal
claim: infra/scout-stack.yml creates log groups but no CloudWatch alarm/SNS/metric-filter resources; /health/ returns a static 200 with no DB/queue check and exists only on the API container (worker and MCP have no health surface and all three Kamal configs are proxy:false with no healthcheck, so a crash-looping container deploys green). Worker/process death manifests only as the absence of log lines and Task Badger events, which nothing converts to an alert; there is no alarm on jobs-doing-older-than-X, active ThreadJobs older than X, ACTIVE schemas with zero tables, or ACTIVE view schemas with missing physical views. Sentry was live before the 2026-06-09 incident and it still ran ~22h — emitted-but-unrouted signals are the demonstrated failure mode and the dead-process variant has the same blindness today; the exact forensic input (last_accessed_at before overwrite) is also unlogged.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced every link.

1. No CloudWatch alarms/SNS/metric-filters: `infra/scout-stack.yml` defines 4 LogGroups (ApiLogGroup/McpLogGroup/WorkerLogGroup/FrontendLogGroup, lines 365-388) and the EC2 IAM policy grants only `logs:CreateLogStream`/`logs:PutLogEvents`/`logs:DescribeLogStreams` (179-181). No `AWS::CloudWatch::Alarm`, `AWS::SNS::*`, or `AWS::Logs::MetricFilter` resource exists. `grep -niE "alarm|sns|metricfilter|AWS::CloudWatch|heartbeat" infra/` returns nothing.

2. Static health check: `apps/workspaces/views.py:8-13` -> `return JsonResponse({"status": "ok"})` with zero DB/queue probing. Routed at `config/urls.py:81` (`path("health/", health_check, ...)`).

3. /health/ only on API: it is a Django route, served by the API container. The frontend Kamal healthcheck (`config/deploy-frontend.yml:45-48`) targets `path: /` (nginx SPA root), not `/health/`.

4. api/worker/mcp proxy:false, no healthcheck: `config/deploy.yml:18` `proxy: false`; `config/deploy-worker.yml:17` `proxy: false`; `config/deploy-mcp.yml:18` `proxy: false`. None contains a `healthcheck:` block. Finding correctly scopes "all three" to these; the 4th config (frontend) is the only one with a proxy+healthcheck.

5. Crash-loop deploys green: `.github/workflows/deploy.yml:73-83` runs `kamal deploy` for mcp/api/worker. Kamal's healthcheck gate lives inside the `proxy:` block, which is absent for all three, so the deploy verifies only that `docker run` succeeded — no readiness/liveness gate.

6. No alarm on the four conditions: the only schedulers are in-worker `@app.periodic` tasks: `expire_inactive_schemas` (cron */30, `tasks.py:516-553`) and `expire_stale_thread_jobs` (cron */15, `tasks.py:819-838`). Both merely reconcile DB state; neither emits an operator signal, and both run inside the very worker whose death is the failure mode. No infra-side EventBridge/Lambda/cron exists (grep of infra empty). No code anywhere checks "ACTIVE schema with zero tables" or "ACTIVE view schema with missing physical views" (grep returns only unrelated hits).

7. Sentry live but blind to dead processes: `config/settings/base.py:296-303` inits `sentry_sdk` when `SENTRY_DSN` set (it is, via every deploy*.yml secret list). It captures exceptions only; a hung/dead process raises nothing. No `capture_message` heartbeat exists (grep empty).

8. Forensic input unlogged: `apps/workspaces/tasks.py:183-184` and `apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:121-122` both overwrite `last_accessed_at = timezone.now()`; neither captures the prior value. schema_manager.py:124-128 logs only the schema name; the tasks.py site logs nothing about the old value.

Reachability: the affected paths are live today — `/health/` is a wired route, the four Kamal configs drive the active production deploy workflow on push to main, and the janitor periodics are the running worker's scheduled tasks. The defect is the *absence* of a detection layer over all of them.
votes.notes: Status DEBT / impact velocity / complexity essential are all accurate — this is a missing-detection-layer gap, not a currently-broken path; it converts silent process-death into ~22h blind windows (matches the 2026-06-09 incident). One minor scoping clarification, not a correction: the finding's "all three Kamal configs are proxy:false with no healthcheck" is precise — there are four Kamal configs total and the frontend one (the public entry) does carry a `/` healthcheck, but that healthcheck only proves nginx serves the SPA and says nothing about API/worker/MCP/DB/queue liveness, so it does not weaken the claim. All file:line citations in the finding verified accurate (scout-stack.yml:360-388, views.py:8-13, taskbadger.py:23, tasks.py:518/585/685 land on the cited constructs).

title: Django-side agent audit log dropped in production (INFO under root WARNING) and logs an always-empty project_id; MCP audit has no actor and is logger-only
claim: The only user/thread-attributed record of agent tool calls is the scout.agent.audit logger at INFO, but production LOGGING configures only django/apps/mcp_server loggers with root WARNING, so the line is suppressed entirely in production; it also logs input_state.get('project_id','') which is always empty (state carries workspace_id since the projects->workspaces rename). The MCP-side tool_context audit covers all tools but context_id is workspace_id only (no user/thread), teardown_schema's destructive body has zero logger calls, the trail is a logger-only stderr line into CloudWatch (30-day), and TODO.md's append-only audit DB table is unchecked. Artifact live queries call execute_query directly outside the MCP tool_context wrapper, so they run N stored SQL statements against tenant data with no audit record at all. Langfuse traces only interactive chat; resume turns and recipe runs are dark.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced every sub-claim:

1. Django-side audit log suppressed in production — CONFIRMED. apps/chat/stream.py:76 `audit_logger = logging.getLogger("scout.agent.audit")`; stream.py:182-188 emits at INFO on every on_tool_end. config/settings/production.py:37-73 LOGGING configures only `django` (WARNING), `apps` (INFO), `mcp_server` (INFO) loggers plus `root` at WARNING. "scout.agent.audit" matches none of the configured prefixes, so it propagates to root where level WARNING drops the INFO record. Reachable: this is the live interactive-chat streaming path (apps/chat/views.py:239 calls langgraph_to_ui_stream).

2. project_id always empty — CONFIRMED. stream.py:187 logs `input_state.get("project_id", "")`, but the only caller builds input_state with `"workspace_id": str(workspace.id)` and no project_id key (apps/chat/views.py:209-215). The resume path also uses workspace_id (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:1144-1150). So even if the log line were emitted, project_id= would always be blank.

3. MCP tool_context audit has no actor — CONFIRMED. mcp_server/envelope.py:90-117 `tool_context(tool_name, context_id, **extra_fields)` logs `tool_call tool=... context_id=... status=... timing_ms=...` to `mcp_server.audit`; context_id is workspace_id at every call site (server.py:117,187,241,302,344,545,662,818). Notably, the agent graph DOES inject user_id and thread_id into every MCP tool call's args (apps/agents/graph/base.py:503-508, 459-470), but only run_materialization declares/consumes them (server.py:523-524) and no tool passes them to tool_context — so the actor is available at the boundary and silently dropped from the audit line. Unlike the Django line, this audit IS emitted in production: the MCP process calls django.setup() (server.py:880-894) which applies the settings LOGGING, and `mcp_server.audit` is a child of the configured `mcp_server` logger at INFO.

4. teardown_schema body — CONFIRMED as stated. server.py:818-865: no logger calls inside the destructive body; dropped schema names go only into the response envelope, not the log. SchemaManager.ateardown/ateardown_view_schema (apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:474-514) log only on role-drop failure (logger.exception), nothing on success. The tool_context wrapper does emit one line (tool=teardown_schema, confirm=True, status) but without what was dropped.

5. Logger-only trail, 30-day retention, TODO unchecked — CONFIRMED. infra/scout-stack.yml:363-387 sets RetentionInDays: 30 on all four CloudWatch log groups. TODO.md:40 has the unchecked item "Append-only audit DB table — MCPAuditLog Django model ... replace logger-only audit trail". No DB-backed audit model exists.

6. Artifact live queries unaudited — CONFIRMED. apps/artifacts/views.py:764-843 ArtifactQueryDataView (routed at apps/artifacts/urls.py:41, live endpoint) loops over artifact.source_queries and calls `execute_query(ctx, sql)` directly (line 822), bypassing tool_context entirely. mcp_server/services/query.py logs only on error paths (lines 102, 113, 132); successful tenant-data queries leave zero log/audit record of user, artifact, or SQL.

7. Langfuse coverage — CONFIRMED with one nuance. get_langfuse_callback/langfuse_trace_context are used only in apps/chat/views.py (grep over apps/). Recipe runs are fully dark: apps/recipes/services/runner.py:313 `graph.ainvoke(initial_state, config)` with no callbacks and no trace context. Resume turns are not 100% dark: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:870-892 `_resume_langfuse_span` opens a single coarse Langfuse observation around the ainvoke (used at tasks.py:1156-1164), but no LangChain CallbackHandler is attached to config, so LLM calls and tool calls inside the resume turn are not traced — the span records only thread_job_id/thread_id/status. For the purpose of the finding (per-tool-call attribution), resume turns remain effectively unobservable.
votes.notes: One overstatement to correct in the finding text: "resume turns ... are dark" in Langfuse is not literally true — _resume_langfuse_span (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:870-892) emits a coarse wrapper span per resume. But it carries no LangChain callback, so no LLM/tool-level spans; for tool-call audit purposes the conclusion stands. Two aggravating details the finding missed: (a) user_id and thread_id are already injected into every MCP tool call's args by the agent graph (apps/agents/graph/base.py:503-508), so the MCP audit line drops actor data it already receives — the fix is nearly free; (b) resume turns and recipe runs also never pass through stream.py's on_tool_end handler (they use ainvoke, not the UI stream), so even fixing the production logger config would not give them the scout.agent.audit line — interactive chat is the only path that ever hits it. Impact classification: this is an observability/audit-trail gap; "correctness" is acceptable but a case could be made for "security" given teardown_schema and tenant-data query attribution.

title: Schema destruction is silent on success: TTL janitor and teardown tasks emit zero log lines for DROP SCHEMA CASCADE
claim: expire_inactive_schemas flips schemas ACTIVE->TEARDOWN and defers drops with no logging (no schema id, last_accessed_at, cutoff, or count); teardown_schema/teardown_view_schema_task log only failures, so a successful DROP SCHEMA CASCADE of data-bearing schemas is unlogged and _fail_dependent_view_schemas' count of broken sibling workspaces is discarded. The exact forensic question of the 2026-06-10 incident — why the janitor dropped a fresh schema — remains unanswerable from emitted logs because the decision input (last_accessed_at) is later overwritten. User-facing degradations (OAuth resolution, thread load, panic-loop escalation, stream timeout) are all logged at WARNING that Sentry's ERROR event-level default and the absent metric filters ignore.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every link from the live scheduled entry point to consequence; all line numbers in the finding are accurate (within 1-2 lines).

1. ENTRY POINT IS LIVE & SCHEDULED. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:516 `@app.periodic(cron="*/30 * * * *")` decorates `expire_inactive_schemas` (line 518). This is a real periodic Procrastinate task running every 30 min, not dead code.

2. SILENT EXPIRY DECISION. expire_inactive_schemas body (tasks.py:535-553) contains ZERO logger calls. It computes `cutoff = timezone.now() - timedelta(hours=settings.SCHEMA_TTL_HOURS)` (SCHEMA_TTL_HOURS=24, config/settings/base.py:345), then `async for schema in TenantSchema.objects.filter(state=ACTIVE, last_accessed_at__lt=cutoff)` flips `schema.state = TEARDOWN`, saves, and defers teardown_schema — emitting no schema id, no last_accessed_at, no cutoff, no count. Same for WorkspaceViewSchema (lines 547-553). Confirmed via `grep -c` and full-body read: no logging anywhere in the function.

3. SILENT SUCCESSFUL DROP. teardown_schema (tasks.py:609-663): the only logger calls are `logger.exception` in the DoesNotExist branch (615), the teardown-failure branch (621 region), and the post-drop EXPIRED-save-failure branch (660). The SUCCESS path (DROP succeeded → flip runs STALE → mark EXPIRED) emits no log line. teardown_view_schema_task (587-606) likewise only `logger.exception` on failure (593, 600). The underlying SchemaManager.teardown (services/schema_manager.py:183-212) executes `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {} CASCADE` (198-201) and logs ONLY in the role-cleanup failure branch (205) — nothing on the successful CASCADE drop of data-bearing schemas. So a successful destruction of a data-bearing tenant schema is unlogged at all three layers.

4. DISCARDED SIBLING-BREAKAGE COUNT. tasks.py:653 `await _fail_dependent_view_schemas(schema.tenant_id)` ignores the return value. _fail_dependent_view_schemas (666-687) is typed `-> int` and ends `return await WorkspaceViewSchema.objects.filter(...).aupdate(state=FAILED)` — the count of broken sibling multi-tenant workspaces is computed and thrown away, unlogged. `grep` confirms line 653 is the only caller.

5. FORENSIC INPUT OVERWRITTEN ON RESURRECTION. The decision input last_accessed_at is overwritten by provision()/resurrect: SchemaManager.provision calls `existing.touch()` (schema_manager.py:77 → models.py:50 sets last_accessed_at=now) and the resurrect/activate path sets `ts.last_accessed_at = timezone.now()` (schema_manager.py:121). So after a fresh schema is dropped and re-provisioned (exactly the 2026-06-10 scenario), the timestamp that drove the janitor's drop decision is gone and unrecoverable from logs. (Nuance: the teardown path itself only updates `state` via update_fields, not last_accessed_at; overwrite requires a subsequent provision/resurrect — but that is precisely what happens on re-materialization.)

6. SENTRY EVENT-LEVEL DEFAULT IGNORES WARNINGS. config/settings/base.py:297-303 `sentry_sdk.init(...)` passes only dsn/environment/release/traces_sample_rate/send_default_pii — NO LoggingIntegration override. `grep` over config/settings/*.py confirms no `LoggingIntegration`, no `integrations=`, no `before_send`. The auto-enabled LoggingIntegration default is event_level=ERROR, so WARNING logs become breadcrumbs, never Sentry events. The production LOGGING dict (production.py:37-73) routes only to a console StreamHandler with no Sentry handler, confirming Sentry capture is purely via the SDK integration. The four cited user-facing degradations are all genuinely WARNING-level: users/signals.py:61 (`logger.warning("No access token available after OAuth...")` plus the three resolve-* exception handlers at 68/73/78 all WARNING), chat/stream.py:213 (`logger.warning("Agent execution timed out after %ds")`), agents/graph/base.py:610 (`logger.warning("agent graph: routing to escalation node...")` — reachable: _should_escalate fires after ESCALATION_TRIGGER_COUNT=3 consecutive tool errors, base.py:88/609). The "thread load" case is chat/thread_views.py:101 `logger.warning("Failed to load checkpoint for thread %s", ..., exc_info=True)` reached via _load_thread_messages used by live thread views (158, 242). All are sub-ERROR and thus dropped by Sentry's default event level.

No metric instrumentation (statsd/prometheus) or log filters exist (grep clean). Status DEBT / impact velocity is correct: nothing is broken at runtime, but the forensic question of the 2026-06-10 incident is genuinely unanswerable from emitted logs.
votes.notes: CONFIRMED with one minor framing nuance, not severe enough to downgrade to PARTIAL. The claim "the decision input (last_accessed_at) is later overwritten" is true only via the resurrect/provision path (schema_manager.py:77 touch() and :121 activate), not by the teardown task itself — teardown_schema's saves use update_fields=["state"] and leave last_accessed_at intact on the TEARDOWN/EXPIRED row. So a dropped-but-not-re-provisioned schema would still retain its decision timestamp in the DB; the overwrite materializes once the tenant is re-materialized (the actual 2026-06-10 flow). This strengthens rather than weakens the finding: the forensic gap is real and the overwrite mechanism exists. Everything else verified exactly as stated. All cited file:line references are accurate. The path is live (every-30-min periodic task) and operates on data-bearing schemas, so reachability is fully confirmed.
